name: Langston Hughes
abstract: James Mercer Langston Hughes (February 1, 1901 – May 22, 1967) was an American poet, social activist, novelist, playwright, and columnist from Joplin, Missouri. An early innovator of jazz poetry, Hughes is best known as a leader of the Harlem Renaissance.

concerts.label: Interval Concert: Music and Poetry of the Negro
Ninth Annual WLIB Festival of Negro Music and Drama

works_created.t: Ask Your Mama: 12 Moods For Jazz

about_quotes.speaker: Earl Turbinton
about_quotes.evidence: a lot of the poetry from the Harlem Renaissance, uh, Gwendolyn Brooks and, uh, Langston Hughes, uh, some other poets. And we proceeded to record some spontaneous improvisation to these, to this poetry as well as some preset type of things that we did to, to, to the poetry.

about_quotes.speaker: Charles Mingus
about_quotes.evidence: Langston Hughes is mentioned only in a bibliographic note by the interviewer; no interaction or personal relationship with Charles Mingus is described in the transcript.

about_quotes.speaker: David N. Baker
about_quotes.evidence: almost always dealt with poetry of black composers, whether it was Paul Laurence Dunbar or Langston Hughes or Mari Evans or Sonia Sanchez or whoever.
about_quotes.doc_title: David N. Baker — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: Delfeayo Marsalis
about_quotes.evidence: We even had a couple of family meetings where she’d read poetry of Langston Hughes about – but we weren’t really that serious about it.
about_quotes.doc_title: Delfeayo Marsalis — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: Helen Humes
about_quotes.evidence: [INTERVIEWER] are still asking for them. Well listen, then you played a part in Langston Hughes' 'Simply Heavenly'. [INTERVIEWEE] Yeah.

about_quotes.speaker: Hentoff Nat
about_quotes.evidence: I enjoyed knowing him very much. I didn’t know him that well, but I knew him... I interviewed him and later got to know him somewhat
about_quotes.doc_title: Hentoff Nat — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History
